elements.sent-2411-001: We improve upon an Omega result due to Soundararajan \cite{Soundararajan_2003 with respect to general trigonometric polynomials having positive Fourier coefficients.}
elements.sent-2411-002: Instead of Dirichlet's approximation theorem we employ the resonance method and this leads to better extreme results in lattice point problems such as Dirichlet's divisor problem and Gauss' circle problem.
elements.sent-2411-003: Moreover, the present approach shows that the resonance method can also be viewed as an additive device, which has been used in multiplicative problems so far.
elements.sent-2411-004: Its extension to trigonometric polynomials with complex coefficients is also discussed and its connection to Bohr and Jessen's proof of Kronecker's theorem is highlighted.
elements.sent-2411-005: For two sequences of real numbers $0\leq\lambda_1<\lambda_2<\dots$ and $f(n)\geq0$, $n\geq1$, with $\sum_{n\geq1 f(n)<\infty$ we consider the trigonometric series \begin{equation}F(x):=\sum_{n\geq1}f(n)\e{\lambda_n x},\quade(x):=e^{2\pi ix},\quad x\inR.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-006: In 2003 Soundararajan \cite[Lemma 2.1]{Soundararajan_2003 showed that, for any integers $L,M,N\geq2$, any set $\mathcal{M}\subseteq\mathbb{N}$ with cardinality $M$ such that $2|\lambda_m-\lambda_N|\leq{\lambda_N}$, $m\in\mathcal{M}$, any $\beta\in\mathbb{R}$ and any $X\geq2$, \begin{equation}\label{motivation} \max_{x\in\brackets*{X/2,(6L)^{M+1}X}}\abs*{\Re\parentheses*{e^{i\beta}F(x)}}\geq\frac{1}{8}\sum_{\substack{m\inM}}f(m)-\frac{4F(0)}{\pi^2X\lambda_N}-\frac{1}{L-1}\sum_{\substack{\lambda_n\leq2\lambda_N}}f(n).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-007: He then employed the above inequality, Voronoi's summation formula and certain information on the anatomy of integers to prove extreme results for Dirichlet's divisor problem and Gauss' circle problem.
elements.sent-2411-008: That is, if \begin{equation}\Delta(x):=\sum_{n\leq x}d(n)-x\log x-(2\gamma-1)x\quadand\quad P(x):=\sum_{n\leq x}r(n)-\pi x,\quad x\geq1,\end{equation} where $d(n)$ denotes the number of divisors of $n$ and $r(n)$ the number of ways of writing $n$ as the sum of two integer squares, then \cite[Theorem 1.1]{Soundararajan_2003 \begin{equation}\label{divisormotiv} \Delta(x)=\Omega\parentheses*{x^{1/4}(\log x)^{1/4}(\log\log x)^{(3/4)(2^{4/3}-1)}(\log\log\log x)^{-5/8}}\end{equation} and \begin{equation}P(x)=\Omega\parentheses*{x^{1/4}(\log x)^{1/4}(\log\log x)^{(3/4)(2^{1/3}-1)}(\log\log\log x)^{-5/8}}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\Delta(x):=\sum_{n\leq x}d(n)-x\log x-(2\gamma-1)x\quadand\quad P(x):=\sum_{n\leq x}r(n)-\pi x,\quad x\geq1,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-009: Before we go any further, let us recall some notation.
elements.sent-2411-010: For a real-valued function $f$ and a positive function $g$, the {\it Omega symbol $f=\Omega(g)$ means that $\limsup_{x\to\infty |f(x)|/g(x)>0$, which is the negation of $f=o(g)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-011: We write $f=\Omega_+(g)$ if $\limsup_{x\to\infty f(x)/g(x)>0$, and $f=\Omega_-(g)$ if $\liminf_{x\to\infty f(x)/g(x)<0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: The Landau symbol $f=O(g)$ and the Vinogradov symbol $f\ll g$ have their usual meaning with the implicit constant being absolute unless indicated otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-013: Lastly, if $f$ is positive, then the notation $f\asymp g$ stands for $g\ll f\ll g$.
elements.sent-2411-014: The $\Omega$-results on $\Delta(x)$ and $P(x)$ are the best known in a line of research initiated by Hardy \cite{Hardy1917 and including most notably the work of Corr\'adi and K\'atai \cite{Corradi1967 and Hafner \cite{Hafner1981 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: In particular, Hafner obtains $\Omega_+$- and $\Omega_-$-results for $\Delta(x)$ and $P(x)$, respectively, with slightly smaller exponents on the $\log\log x$ and observes that this is the limit of his method.
elements.sent-2411-016: On the other hand, Soundararajan's approach has its limit rooted in the inequality \eqref{motivation and specifically in the last term on the right-hand side.}
elements.sent-2411-017: For applications one is led to consider the parameter $L$ as an increasing function of $X$ in order for this term to be negligible.
elements.sent-2411-018: At the same time we aim to maximize $M$ so that the first term in the right-hand side of \eqref{motivation is as large as possible while keeping the interval $[X/2,(6L)^{M+1 X]$ of polynomial length.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: As a consequence the parameter $M$ can be at most $o(\log X)$ and removing this restriction has been the motivation for this work.
elements.sent-2411-020: The above error term yields the improvement on the magnitude of $M$ since the choice of $Y=o(X/\log X)$ and $M\ll\log X$ is admissible and the factor $Y(M+\log X)X^{-1}$ acts like ${(L-1) ^{-1}$.}
elements.sent-2411-021: We can therefore take $M\asymp\log X$ while keeping the interval, where the extremal behavior of $F(x)$ is detected, of polynomial length.
elements.sent-2411-022: In contrast to the aforementioned results of Hardy, Hafner and Soundararajan who had employed Dirichlet's simultaneous approximation theorem, Theorem \ref{keyresult will follow by an application of the {\it resonance method .}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Incidentally, this technique has been developed by Soundararajan \cite{Soundararajan2008 a few years after \cite{Soundararajan_2003 to address questions related to the extreme values of the Riemann zeta-function $\zeta(s)$, $s:=\sigma+it$, on the critical line\footnote{Similar results and the development of the resonance method inside the strip $1/2 < \sigma < 1$ have been carried out earlier -yet passed unknown for decades- by Voronin \cite{Voronin1988 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-024: The idea is, roughly speaking, to prove $\Omega$-results for a Dirichlet polynomial (with multiplicative coefficients) $F(t)=\sum_{n\leq N f(n)n^{it}$, by introducing another Dirichlet polynomial $R(t)$ that will {\it resonate with $F(t)$ in the following sense: \begin{equation}\max_{t\in[Y,X]}|F(t)|\int_Y^X|R(t)|^2dt\geq\abs*{\int_Y^XF(t)|R(t)|^2dt}\geq A\int_Y^X|R(t)|^2dt,\quad A>0.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Then it is a matter of properly constructing $R(t)$ to maximise $A$ while satisfying the above inequalities.
elements.sent-2411-026: Of course several error terms are involved in the background but when successfully estimated, the method yields also lower bounds for the measure of $t\in[Y,X]$ at which $|F(t)|$ is large.
elements.sent-2411-027: We will first extend the resonance method to the setting of general trigonometric series $F(x)$ having positive Fourier coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-028: This assumption not only simplifies the exposition but it also produces superior results compared to when $f(n)\in\mathbb{C}$.
elements.sent-2411-029: Now the resonator $R(x)$ will not be a Dirichlet polynomial but a trigonometric polynomial whose frequencies are linear combinations of the ``building blocks'' $\lambda_{m}$, $m\in\mathcal{M}$.
elements.sent-2411-030: The proof of Theorem \ref{keyresult distinctly illustrates how $R(x)$ works to bring forth the terms $f(\lambda_{m )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: In the special case when $\beta=0$ $(\bmod\,2\pi)$ we can also refine the theorem and obtain lower bounds for the measure of those $x\in[Y,2^MX]$ at which $\Re(F(x))$ is large.
elements.sent-2411-032: If $\Sigma>0$, then for any $0<V\leq\Sigma$ \begin{equation}meas\set*{x\in\brackets*{Y,2^MX}:\Re\parentheses*{F(x)}\geq V}\geq\frac{(\Sigma-V) X}{2 (M\log 2+\log X)\max_{x\in\brackets*{Y,2^MX}}|F(x)|}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-033: The resonator constructed in the sequel is modeled from \cite{Aistleitner_2015 and it consist of terms with ``square-free'' frequencies (we will give a precise meaning of this).}
elements.sent-2411-034: More involved resonators can produce smaller constants.
elements.sent-2411-035: For instance a resonator modeled from \cite{Aistleitner2017a would give $1$ instead of $1/4$ in Theorem \ref{lowerbounds .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-036: On the one hand, we are free to choose any terms from $F(x)$ with the natural choice being the first terms of the descending sequence $\max_{n\geq1 f(n)=f(m_0)\geq f(m_1)\geq f(m_2)\geq\dots$, the order of which is often not easy to determine.}
elements.sent-2411-037: The distribution of the frequencies $\lambda_n$ on the real line has no effect on this selection, which is the advantage of working with positive coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-038: On the other hand, we can only consider at most $O(\log X)$ of these terms because of the factor $2^M$, which represents in this case the approximate number of terms the resonator suffices to consist of in order to ``capture'' $M$ terms from $F(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-039: Subject to these restrictions, we can obtain by implementing Theorem \ref{keyresult to the proof of \cite[Theorem 1.1]{Soundararajan_2003 the following corollary.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In Section \ref{applications we will briefly see how to obtain it, since the proof of \cite[Thorem 1.1]{Soundararajan_2003 remains practically the same.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: We provide several other examples where Theorem \ref{keyresult and Theorem \ref{lowerbounds can be used instead to obtain improved lower bounds but the list is definitely not complete.}}
elements.sent-2411-042: In the special case when $\lambda_n=\log n$ and $f(n)$ is a multiplicative function, Theorem \ref{keyresult and Theorem \ref{lowerbounds do not represent the known $\Omega$-results for $F(x)$, because this is not the right polynomial to consider.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: The theorems should instead be applied to a Dirichlet polynomial approximation of $\log F(x)$ whose frequencies are given by $\lambda_{p^k =k\log p$ for prime $p$ and integer $k\geq1$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: If a resonator produces an extreme value $A>1$ when applied to $|F(x)|$, then the same resonator produces the extreme value $\log A$ when applied to $\log |F(x)|$ and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-046: In both cases the resonator is constructed in such way to interact with the same terms, namely $f({p^k )\mathrm{e (\lambda_{p^k x)$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: However, due to the multiplicativity of $f(n)$ and the additivity of $\lambda_n$, the remaining terms of $F(x)$ can also give a positive contribution to the lower bound which is is then mutliplicatively reconstructed from the contribution coming from $f({p^k )$.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Of course the resonance method should be applied to $\log|F(x)|$ with greater care because we have to take into account the zero-distribution of $F(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-049: If we have sufficiently knowledge about this distribution then the two approaches are in sync.
elements.sent-2411-050: For instance, the resonator employed in the sequel reduces to the one constructed by Aistleitner in \cite{Aistleitner_2015 for proving lower bounds of $\zeta(s)$ to the right of the critical line.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Applying the same resonator to $\log|\zeta(s)|$, in the sense of Theorem \ref{keyresult , we could obtain the corresponding lower bound.}
elements.sent-2411-052: With additional information about $f(p^k)$, it is also possible to construct resonators with a richer structure than the one used in the present work, leading to better lower bounds.
elements.sent-2411-053: Such constructions have been first realized by Soundararajan \cite{Soundararajan2008 and are culminating in the work of Bondarenko and Seip \cite{Bondarenko2017 and de la Bretèche and Tenenbaum \cite{Breteche2018 regarding lower bounds of $\zeta(s)$ on the critical line.}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: It is also possible to generalize the resonance method to trigonometric polynomials having complex Fourier coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-055: In this case, however, the linear dependencies of the frequencies have also an effect on the length of the interval where $\Re(F(x))$ reaches a certain magnitude.
elements.sent-2411-056: This is a natural by-product of the method and a common feature with the quantitative version of Kronecker's approximation theorem.
elements.sent-2411-057: It turns out that the resonance method is one of the many techniques that have been devised to prove Kronecker's theorem and a result for general trigonometric polynomials in the form of Theorem \ref{keyresult has been already obtained by Chen \cite{Chen2000 .}}

elements.sent-2411-059: In the multiplicative setting we remark that this is the reason why we are able to obtain $\Omega$-results of similar strength when applying Soundararajan's or Chen's resonator to an $L$-function $L(s)$ or its logarithm $\log L(s)$, respectively, whose Dirichlet coefficients satisfy certain assumptions of number-theoretic interest.
elements.sent-2411-060: In \cite{Aistleitner2017 Aistleitner and Pa{\'n kowski employ both approaches/resonators and make a detailed discussion about their strong and weak points.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: They note that Chen's theorem produces inferior results only on the {\it critical line of $L(s)$.}
elements.sent-2411-062: This is because Soundararajan's resonator is constructed from many more and much larger primes and it is tailor-made to capture this transition phase of the extremal behaviour of $L(s)$ from the right of its critical line to the critical line.
elements.sent-2411-063: If we were to apply this resonator to $\log |L(s)|$ or $\Im(\log L(s))$ then we could recover the expected lower bound as we discussed above.
elements.sent-2411-064: Indeed this can be easily confirmed under the Riemann hypothesis for $L(s)$, namely that $L(s)$ has no zeros on the right of its critical line.

elements.sent-2411-111: The origins of it can be traced back to Bohr and Jessen's proof \cite{Bohr1932 of Kronecker's approximation theorem.}
elements.sent-2411-112: For any real numbers $\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_N$ and $\varepsilon>0$, there is $x_0\in\mathbb{R}$, such that\footnote{Here $\norm{x}$ denotes the distance of $x$ from the nearest integer.. \begin{equation}\max_{n\leq N}\norm{x_0\lambda_n-\alpha_n}<\varepsilon.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-113: It is clear that the maximum is less than or equal to $N+1$ but to obtain the equality they employed the positive trigonometric polynomial \begin{equation}W_L(x):=K_L(x\lambda_1-\alpha_1)\cdots K_L(x\lambda_N-\alpha_N),\quad K_L(x):=\sum_{|\ell|\leq L}\parentheses*{1-\frac{|\ell|}{L}}e(\ell x).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-114: Multiplying out and using the linear independency of the frequencies it can be shown that \begin{equation}F(x)W_L(x)=1+\frac{L-1}{L}N+S(x),\end{equation} where $S(x)$ is a trigonometric polynomial whose frequencies are all different from $0$.
elements.sent-2411-115: They concluded their argument by proving that \begin{equation}\label{Kronecker}  \max_{x\inR}\Re(F(x)) \geq\parentheses*{\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^TW_L(x)dx}^{-1}\Re\parentheses*{\lim_{T\to\infty}\frac{1}{2T}\int_{-T}^TF(x)W_L(x)dx} =1\cdot\parentheses*{1+\frac{L-1}{L}N} \end{equation} and then taking $L$ to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-116: Note that $W(x)$ acts as a ``normalized'' resonating polynomial for $F(x)$ and this can also be seen through its expansion \begin{equation}W_L(x)=\sum_{\mu\inA_L}c(\mu)e(\mu x),\quadA_L:=\set*{\sum_{n\leq N}\ell_n\lambda_n:|\ell_n|\leq L},\quad c(\mu)\in[0,1].\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-117: Replacing $c(\mu)$ with suitable coefficients $\tilde{c (\mu)\geq0$ would yield a trigonometric polynomial $|R_L(x)|^2$ which does the exact same job as $W_L(x)$ in \eqref{Kronecker .}}
elements.sent-2411-118: The limits will be different and the ratio will be $(N+1)L/(L+1)$, but when taking $L$ to infinity the same result will follow.
elements.sent-2411-119: There is a rich literature on quantitative versions of Kronecker's theorem, that is find explicitly a $T$ such that the theorem holds for some $x_0\leq T$, and we refer to the work of Gonek and Montgomery \cite{Gonek2016 for a detailed exposition and historical review.}
elements.sent-2411-120: One of the techniques is naturally based on not taking $T$ to infinity in \eqref{Kronecker and estimating instead the least $T_0$ such that the occurring error terms when divided by $T_0$ would be $\ll 1+N(L-1)/L$.}
